note: Matthew names Isaiah directly, reading the virgin-born child as fulfilled in Jesus.
The chief priests quote Micah to Herod to identify Bethlehem as the Messiah’s birthplace.
Matthew cites Zechariah’s king-on-a-donkey oracle as fulfilled at the triumphal entry.
John notes the soldiers dividing Jesus’s garments and casting lots fulfilled this psalm.
Peter draws directly on the Suffering Servant’s wounds to describe Christ bearing sin.
Peter’s Pentecost sermon quotes this psalm as David foreseeing the resurrection.
Matthew identifies John the Baptist as the voice crying in the wilderness Isaiah foretold.
Mark opens his Gospel by quoting Malachi’s messenger promise as fulfilled in John the Baptist.
Matthew reads Hosea’s "out of Egypt" line as fulfilled in the holy family’s return from Egypt.
Matthew ties Jeremiah’s Rachel-weeping image to Herod’s slaughter of the infants.
Matthew connects the thirty pieces of silver thrown to the potter with Judas’s betrayal price.
Peter tells the council that the rejected stone of this psalm is the risen Jesus.
Jesus reads this very passage aloud in the Nazareth synagogue and claims it for himself.
Peter tells the Pentecost crowd that the Spirit’s outpouring is exactly what Joel foretold.
Jesus quotes David’s psalm to press the point that the Messiah is David’s Lord, not merely his son.
Matthew cites Isaiah’s light-in-darkness oracle as fulfilled when Jesus began preaching in Galilee.
John cites Zechariah’s "look on him whom they pierced" as fulfilled at the crucifixion.
John records Jesus given sour wine on the cross, echoing this psalm of the suffering righteous one.
John notes that none of Jesus’s bones were broken, as with the Passover lamb.
Jesus applies Isaiah’s warning about a dull-hearted people to those who hear his parables unmoved.
Paul builds his gospel argument on the singular "seed" promised to Abraham, meaning Christ.
Matthew cites the Servant’s borne griefs as fulfilled when Jesus healed the sick.
Peter echoes Isaiah’s sinless, deceit-free Servant in describing Christ’s innocence.
Philip explains this very passage to the Ethiopian official as speaking of Jesus’s silent suffering.
Matthew quotes God’s chosen, Spirit-anointed Servant as fulfilled in Jesus’s quiet ministry.
James quotes Amos at the Jerusalem council to defend Gentiles being welcomed into the rebuilt house of David.
Peter tells the crowd that Moses’s promised prophet like himself is Jesus.
Paul quotes this psalm at Antioch as fulfilled in God raising Jesus up.
The Jerusalem church quotes this psalm of raging kings as fulfilled in the plot against Jesus.
Jesus quotes this psalm about a trusted friend’s betrayal just before naming Judas.
Jesus cites this psalm’s "hated without cause" line as fulfilled in the world’s hatred of him.
Peter quotes this psalm about a vacated office as the basis for replacing Judas among the apostles.
Jesus quotes Isaiah’s lip-service worship line against the Pharisees’ empty tradition.
Peter cites Isaiah’s stone of stumbling as the fate of those who reject Christ.
Paul quotes Isaiah’s tested cornerstone as fulfilled in Christ, a stone some stumble over.
Paul says Isaiah boldly foretold God being found by those who never sought him, namely the Gentiles.
Paul warns his hearers at Antioch with Habakkuk’s words about a work they would not believe.
Paul grounds his gospel of faith on Habakkuk’s "the righteous will live by faith."
Jesus points to Jonah’s three days in the fish as a sign of his own coming burial and resurrection.
Jesus quotes Zechariah’s struck shepherd and scattered sheep to predict the disciples fleeing.
Jesus says Isaiah’s line about being counted among criminals must be fulfilled in him.
John cites Isaiah’s lament that his report went unbelieved to explain the crowd’s unbelief.
Paul applies the law’s curse on one hung on a tree to Christ’s crucifixion.
Jesus identifies John the Baptist as the promised Elijah of Malachi’s closing prophecy.
Paul quotes Isaiah’s root of Jesse as the one in whom Gentiles would hope.
Paul quotes Moses’s song about a foolish nation to explain Gentile inclusion provoking Israel.
Hebrews quotes God’s promise to be a father to David’s heir as spoken of the Son.
Hebrews applies this royal psalm’s "your throne, O God" directly to the Son.
Hebrews quotes this psalm’s creation language as addressed to the eternal Son.
Hebrews quotes this psalm on humanity’s dignity as fulfilled in the Son made lower than angels for a while.
Hebrews puts this psalm’s "a body you prepared for me" in the mouth of Christ entering the world.
Paul calls this promise to Abraham the gospel preached in advance to the nations.
Paul quotes Isaiah’s promise that death is swallowed up as fulfilled in the resurrection.
Paul taunts death with Hosea’s own words after affirming the resurrection.
John’s note that not one of Jesus’s bones was broken also echoes this psalm of the righteous sufferer.
Jesus tells the high priest he will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds, as Daniel foresaw.
